figures.caption: Figure 2: Efficient Domain-Adaptive Policy Learning via Kernel Representation. The framework is composed of offline and online phases. During the offline training phase, we randomly sample kernel coefficients and bandwidth parameters to generate a rich diversity of disturbance profiles. We then optimize the control policy via differentiable simulation with analytical gradients, a process that takes only 50 seconds of training time on an RTX 4090 GPU. During hardware deployment, the policy adapts to non-stationary environments in real time by updating both the kernel coefficients and bandwidth parameters simultaneously through online least-squares estimation.

Figure 4: Evolution of α Under Different Disturbance in Section 4.3. All 75 elements of α are shown and each curve represents an averaged element of α. All elements of α lie within 3 times standard deviation of the Gaussian distribution N (0, 1 2I) used in training.

Figure 5: Results of Aerodynamic Secnario in the Numerical Simulations in Section 4.3. Top: Tracking error (m), with the inserted plot showing RMSE (m). Middle: Norm of residual prediction error (m/s2), with the inserted plot showing the average error (m/s2). Bottom: Ground-truth disturbances (m/s2).

Figure 6: Results of Aerodynamic + Sinusoidal Secnario in the Numerical Simulations in Section 4.3. Top: Tracking error (m), with the inserted plot showing RMSE (m). Middle: Norm of residual prediction error (m/s2), with the inserted plot showing the average error (m/s2). Bottom: Ground-truth disturbances (m/s2).

Figure 7: Results of Aerodynamic + Switching Secnario in the Numerical Simulations in Section 4.3. Top: Tracking error (m), with the inserted plot showing RMSE (m). Middle: Norm of residual prediction error (m/s2), with the inserted plot showing the average error (m/s2). Bottom: Ground-truth disturbances (m/s2).

Figure 8: Results of Aerodynamic + Quadratic-Phase Sinusoidal Secnario in the Numerical Simulations in Section 4.3. Top: Tracking error (m), with the inserted plot showing RMSE (m). Middle: Norm of residual prediction error (m/s2), with the inserted plot showing the average error (m/s2). Bottom: Ground-truth disturbances (m/s2).

Figure 9: Sample Trajectory of Hardware Experiment under Ground Effect in Section 4.4. Each subplot shows the x−, y−, z− position and the reference trajectory (dashed) over a 20s evaluation window. Our method achieves the best tracking performance in RMSE.

Figure 10: Sample Trajectory of Hardware Experiment under Wind Disturbance in Section 4.4. Each subplot shows the x−, y−, z− position and the reference trajectory (dashed) over a 20s evaluation window. Our method achieves the best tracking performance in RMSE.

Figure 11: Sample Trajectory of Hardware Experiment under Suspended Payload in Section 4.4. Each subplot shows the x−, y−, z− position and the reference trajectory (dashed) over a 20s evaluation window. Our method achieves the best tracking performance in RMSE.
